title: can / could / be able to
examples: >> I've taught a lot of English now to kids, to adults, to company workers. I I hear this a lot. I can understand it when I read it. When people speak slowly to me, I can understand it. >> I was doing a lot of research and the reason for this is because humans speak in the easiest way possible. Vow, consonant, vow, consonant, vow, consonant. It's like a sandwich. Bread, meat, bread. Native speakers say, "I'm going to." Another example is like in Spider-Man, we see Peter Parker. He's talking to Iron Man and he says really fast, "I just wanted to be like you. My whole goal now is to help people to make the connection. I know these words. How can I connect that with what's actually being pronounced? A lot of Koreans learn English from a very young age. title: have to / must
examples: >> Of course, you have to eat with kimchi.

>> So, I do that in three ways. First thing, I have just a list of the most common connected speech phrases, sentences. For example, like going to would switch to going to, have to would switch to have to. And so in my YouTube videos and in my course, I just go through the most common connected speech things and I give you several examples.

Notice that they're actually saying, "Did you have lunch yet?" Right? Not did you. Did you? And the last thing is vocabulary. Now, vocabulary isn't something we can ignore completely. We have to still continue to upgrade our vocabulary because there's a difference between textbook vocabulary and real spoken vocabulary. For example, when you say bye to someone, goodbye. Native speakers actually do say goodbye. But we also say, "Have a good one. Take care.
